text: The allegory of the cave has a structural feature that is easy to overlook: the prisoners are not simply ignorant — they are chained. Their orientation is constrained by something external before any internal failure occurs. This detail matters philosophically. Plato is acknowledging that attention is not purely a matter of individual will; it is shaped by the architecture of one's environment. The designers of the cave — those who carry objects before the fire — are not named or blamed, but they are present. To turn toward the light requires not only inner resolve but, in many cases, a change in external conditions that makes turning possible.
question: What structural detail of the cave allegory does the passage argue is easy to overlook?

options.text: That the fire is positioned behind the prisoners rather than in front
That the shadows on the wall are cast by specific objects
That the prisoners are chained — their orientation is externally constrained before any internal failure
That only one prisoner is ever freed, not all of them
